_design: Object-centric knowledge graph with 13 layers (0-12). 1 Read = full self-aware intelligence. Layer 0 (core_principles) added Session #054 — constitutional tier above rules. Layer 11 (incidents) added Session #050 to fix 'context loaded but not used' failure mode.

core_principles.name: Knowledge over velocity
core_principles.statement: Nunca sacrificar trazabilidad o conocimiento por velocidad. Velocidad no es conocimiento. Perder trazabilidad es irreversible; ser lento es reversible.
core_principles.rationale: El valor acumulado del brain es su capacidad de citar, falsificar y razonar sobre incidentes futuros. Un pipeline que olvida un matiz gana velocidad aparente pero pierde capacidad de decisión. El failure mode es invisible en el turno actual y catastrófico en el siguiente. Session #053 identificó 16 follow-up items committed en retro que nunca se formalizaron en PMO — ejemplo directo de velocidad sobre conocimiento.
how_to_apply: Antes de comprimir, resumir o archivar cualquier información persistida, preguntar: '¿esto pierde una pieza de evidencia o un matiz causal?'. Si sí, no comprimir.
Terseness en conversación con el usuario != terseness en el brain. El brain siempre preserva; la conversación puede ser corta porque el brain está detrás.
Al escribir retros, incidentes, claims: lista completa de fallos con link causal, nunca bullets sin razón.
Superseded claims: enlazar superseded_by_claim_id + superseded_reason, no solo flag.
Al hacer triage de blind_spots/KUs/DQ: clasificar, nunca eliminar.
Si dudo entre 'más corto' y 'más trazable', elijo trazable.

core_principles.name: Preserve first, context is cheap
core_principles.statement: Con 1M context (Opus 4.6), tamaño no es constraint. El bottleneck real es findability, que se resuelve con estructura, no con compresión. Compresión lossy solo ocurre en query-time, nunca en storage-time.
core_principles.rationale: Brain v2 heredó mental model de modelos con 200K context donde size mattered. Ese constraint desapareció. Hoy la única optimización legítima al almacenar es estructurar (listas tipadas, campos separados, schemas explícitos). Cualquier compresión destructiva al persistir es technical debt irreversible.
how_to_apply: Never concatenate evidencia distinta en un string — siempre lista tipada con type/ref/cite.
Never summarize annotations de objetos — cada annotation es un hallazgo atómico, se añade, no se funde.
Never retire known_unknowns por antigüedad — solo cuando se responden con answer + resolution_session.
Never drop blind_spots sin clasificar (MISSING / GHOST / PSEUDO).
Never compress source code validation chains — cada línea cited es un anchor irreemplazable.
Compresión ocurre en query-time (resumen on-the-fly para el usuario), nunca en storage-time (destrucción del original).

structure_vs_prose_rule.use_structure_when: campo contiene entidades repetibles (fuentes, objetos, IDs, referencias)
structure_vs_prose_rule.use_prose_when: campo contiene razonamiento causal o narrativa conectiva (rule.why, annotation.finding)
structure_vs_prose_rule.example_prose: rules.why = 'During INC-000006313 Part 2 cleanup, DBS almost added X instead of Y because...'

core_principles.name: Precision, evidence, facts
core_principles.statement: Toda decisión, claim o recomendación del agente debe estar anclada en máxima precisión posible + evidencia verificable + facts comprobables. Opinión sin evidencia no es análisis. Aproximación sin medir no es precisión.
core_principles.rationale: Sin este principio el agente genera plausibility (texto que suena correcto) en vez de knowledge (texto que cita fuentes). Plausibility es indistinguible de hallucination para el usuario. El único antídoto es exigir evidencia y tier explícito antes de afirmar. Error concreto session #054: dije '34 PERNRs' en vez del conteo real (11) — aproximación sin medir.
how_to_apply: Antes de afirmar: ¿tengo fuente citable (path:line, query, incident ID)? Si no, digo 'no verificado' o pregunto.
Todo claim debe tener tier: TIER_1 (source code + data) / TIER_2 (empirical) / TIER_3 (inferred).
Antes de cerrar incidente: cadena completa de validación con anchors file:line, no resumen.
Números exactos, no aproximaciones. '11 PERNRs', no '~30 PERNRs'.
Fuentes con path + línea: 'extracted_code/CLAS/X/CM004:line_8', no 'en la clase X'.
Antes de una recomendación: ¿qué evidencia la sostiene? ¿qué la falsificaría? Si no puedo responder, no es recomendación, es especulación.

rules.rule: RFC_READ_TABLE has a 512-byte line buffer limit. When total field widths exceed this, SAP returns TABLE_WITHOUT_DATA or DATA_BUFFER_EXCEEDED.
rules.why: Fixed MAX_FIELDS_PER_CALL=8 works for most tables but fails on ESSR (which has very wide session description fields). Even 5 fields overflow the buffer.
rules.how_to_apply:
`rfc_helpers.py` now auto-detects the right chunk size:
1. Try 8 fields -> if TABLE_WITHOUT_DATA, halve to 4
2. Try 4 fields -> if still fails, halve to 2
3. If even 2 fields fail -> genuinely empty or auth issue

This is in `rfc_read_paginated()` in the split-field section. The adaptive logic runs once per table per WHERE clause, then uses that chunk size for all pages.

Additional learnings:
- ESSR max fields = 4 (LOESSION field is ~200 bytes alone)
- BSIS/BSAS max fields = 8 (works fine with 28 fields split into 4 chunks)
- EKPO max fields = 8 (19 fields, 3 chunks)
- BKPF max fields = ALL (12 fields fit in one call, no split needed)

rules.rule: VPN drops are normal during long SAP extractions at UNESCO. Without auto-reconnect, each drop kills the extraction thread and remaining months get 0-row checkpoints (which block retries).
rules.why: BSIS extraction (2.29M rows, ~2 hours) required 4+ restarts due to VPN drops. Each restart was manual and lost time.
rules.how_to_apply:
- `get_connection("P01")` returns a `ConnectionGuard` (not raw pyrfc.Connection)
- ConnectionGuard.call() catches connection-closed/timeout/CPIC errors
- Retries up to 3 times with escalating wait (10s, 20s, 30s)
- Transparent to callers -- rfc_read_paginated works unchanged

Error substrings that trigger reconnect:
- "connection closed", "partner not reached", "timeout"
- "communication failure", "CPIC_", "RFC_COMMUNICATION_FAILURE"
- "RFC_INVALID_HANDLE", "connection has been closed"

Pair with error-safe checkpoints: if a period errors with 0 rows, do NOT write checkpoint file. This allows the period to be retried on next run.

rules.rule: User wants AUTONOMOUS execution. Stop asking for permission or confirmation before running bash commands.
rules.why: Every session the user says "execute autonomously" and then Claude still asks "can I run this?" or "should I proceed?". This wastes time and frustrates the user.
rules.how_to_apply:
1. NEVER ask "can I run this command?" — just run it
2. NEVER ask "should I proceed?" — just proceed
3. NEVER ask "is the VPN connected?" — just try the connection and report results
4. If a command fails, fix it and retry — don't ask what to do
5. If P01 connection fails, say "P01 unreachable — need VPN" and stop (don't ask)

rules.rule: Programs and tables are the system. Skills, companions, knowledge docs are interpretation. The Brain must center on code→table→config relationships as the backbone.
rules.why: Session #048 INC-000006073 took hours tracing LHRTSF01→PA0027→ZXTRVU05→GGB1→KTOKK→AKONT manually. If the Brain had program→table→config edges as its core, a single impact query would have shown the entire chain in seconds. The 52K fund nodes and 7K transport nodes are bulk — the ~1,200 code nodes and ~120 table nodes with their relationships are where the real intelligence lives.
rules.how_to_apply:
1. Brain rebuild (H34) must prioritize: code nodes, table nodes, config nodes, and the edges between them
2. Edge types that matter most:
   - CALLS (program → program/FM)
   - READS_TABLE (program → table)
   - JOINS_WITH (table → table)
   - CONFIGURES (config table → program behavior)
   - DETERMINES (data field → data field, e.g., KTOKK→AKONT)
   - DERIVES_FIELD (program:line → field value)
   - ROOT_CAUSED_BY (incident → code:line + config)
3. Auto-parser covers CALLS + READS_TABLE. Manual edges needed for CONFIGURES, DETERMINES, DERIVES_FIELD.
4. Every incident resolution should produce new edges — INC-000006073 produced 48 edges. These compound over time.
5. The Brain is valuable when it answers "what happens if X changes?" — that requires program→table→config as backbone.

rules.rule: Never extract BSEG cluster table directly via RFC_READ_TABLE. It has severe limitations:
rules.why: BSEG is a cluster table that references 6 transparent secondary index tables. Extracting the cluster is 10-50x slower and misses fields.
rules.how_to_apply:
Use the Celonis replacement pattern:

| Table | Content | KOART | Filter |
|-------|---------|-------|--------|
| BSIK | Vendor open items | K | BUDAT (NOT CPUDT) |
| BSAK | Vendor cleared items | K | BUDAT (NOT CPUDT) |
| BSID | Customer open items | D | BUDAT (NOT CPUDT) |
| BSAD | Customer cleared items | D | BUDAT (NOT CPUDT) |
| BSIS | GL open items | S | BUDAT (NOT CPUDT) |
| BSAS | GL cleared items | S | BUDAT (NOT CPUDT) |

rules.rule: BSEG is NOT a table we extract or enrich. Stop treating it as one.
rules.why: BSEG is a cluster table. The secondary indexes (BSIS/BSAS etc.) ARE the data. The Golden Query covers 85.9% WBS via OBJNRZ. Enriching from BSEG = waste.
rules.how_to_apply: When someone says "BSEG field is missing", first check if it's available via Golden Query JOIN. Only if genuinely unavailable from any joined table should you consider BSEG — and even then, it's low priority.

rules.rule: BSEG CAN be read via RFC_READ_TABLE in P01. It has been declustered (converted to transparent table).
rules.why: We assumed BSEG was a cluster table and couldn't be extracted. It's been declustered in this system. The TABLE_WITHOUT_DATA error was caused by including MANDT in the filter.
rules.how_to_apply: When you need WBS element, extract PROJK from BSEG using document keys, then JOIN PRPS.PSPNR = PROJK to get POSID + POST1.

rules.rule: When discovering a new system in the landscape, NEVER guess its purpose from the hostname.
rules.why: Session #031 misclassified BCU as "Business Connector" (was actually Budget Control System), missed that MuleSoft was middleware (not the target system — Core Manager/Planner are), and didn't find SuccessFactors because it connects via HTTP/ICF not RFC.

rules.rule: When building visual companions (diagrams, dashboards, analysis pages), ALWAYS follow this order:
rules.why: Session #031 rebuilt the connectivity diagram 4 times because we kept discovering missing systems (7→21→31→38). Each rebuild wasted 10-15 minutes. If we'd analyzed all 239 RFC destinations + DBCON + TMSCSYS + TFDIR first, we'd have built the diagram once.
rules.how_to_apply:
- For system diagrams: query RFCDES + DBCON + TMSCSYS + EDIDC + ICFSERVICE + TFDIR BEFORE drawing anything
- For any companion: collect ALL data, classify it, THEN build HTML
- Never classify a system from hostname alone — check CTS objects, jobs, and code references

rules.rule: ALL reads = P01 (prod). Only exception: code deployment writes = D01 (dev).
rules.why: D01 may have untransported config, draft trees, or test entries that don't exist in P01. Only P01 reflects what is real and in production. Session #039 error: ran DMEE tree probe against D01 because P01 was unreachable — user correctly rejected the results because D01 config is not trustworthy for analysis.
rules.how_to_apply: If P01 is unreachable, WAIT. Do not fall back to D01 for reads. The only valid D01 operation is writing code. When P01 is down, work on offline tasks (skill docs, analysis of already-extracted data, scripts for later execution).

rules.rule: Data scope = 2024 to 2026 ONLY.
rules.why: Pre-2024 data is historical context, not actionable. The user wants current-state analysis, not archaeology.
rules.how_to_apply: When extracting, enriching, or analyzing data, filter to 2024-2026. Don't spend time investigating pre-2024 anomalies unless explicitly asked. Transport analysis is the only exception where longer periods are needed.

rules.rule: Rule: Decision Logs Required for Destructive Actions
rules.why:
